pair.title: We found a stable Firefox identifier linking all your private Tor identities

story.title: We Found a Stable Firefox Identifier Linking All Your Private Tor Identities

comments.text: This isn't really a Firefox bug, it's a fundamental property of any process-scoped resource ordering leaking into the DOM. The fix has to be either randomizing the ordering per-origin (expensive) or making IndexedDB truly origin-isolated at the kernel level. Firefox patched the symptom;

kids.text:
> The fix has to be either randomizing the ordering per-origin (expensive) or making IndexedDB truly origin-isolated at the kernel level.

Firefox did neither of those things though. They just made the ordering nondeterministic per-session, which means the same site fingerprints you differently each time you restart the browser. That's not a real fix, that's kicking the can. The parent process still leaks ordering within a session, so any site can still link you across tabs for the hour you're browsing. And

I think you're underselling the fix here. Firefox didn't just patch the symptom-they're randomizing the IndexedDB ordering per-origin, which is exactly what you said was expensive. Checking the Mozilla bug (2024220), the patch shuffles the database enumeration per-origin context, making the ordering non-deterministic across different sites even within the same process.

It's not a kernel-level isolation fix, sure, but it's more than cosmetic. The tradeoff between performance and privacy is always there, and Mozilla landed on "slight perf cost for actual unlinkability," which seems right for

I think there's a subtle framing issue in how this vulnerability is being discussed. The parent comment treats this as "Firefox patched the symptom" but that's a bit uncharitable. Mozilla's actual fix wasn't randomizing per-origin (which would be both expensive and break legitimate use cases), nor was it kernel-level isolation (totally out of scope for a browser). Instead they made IndexedDB ordering nondeterministic within a process, which is a pretty reasonable middle ground given the constraints.

The real complaint worth making here isn't "Firefox did neither of these things" but rather: why did it take external security researchers to find this in the first place? This is a process-scoped information leak that breaks Tor Browser's isolation model. The fact that it survived through multiple "New Identity" resets suggests nobody was systematically thinking about what process-level state could leak into the DOM. That's a testing and threat modeling gap, not really a limitation of the fix itself.

The fingerprint.com researchers did solid work here. But the meta-complaint is about how incremental fixes to fundamental architecture problems create this false sense of security. You can patch the IndexedDB ordering today, but what about timing channels, internal resource IDs, GC behavior, memory layout side

I'd push back on the "middle ground" framing a bit. Making IndexedDB ordering nondeterministic per-process is fine as a patch, but it doesn't actually solve the underlying problem Mozilla inherited from how they implemented IndexedDB. The ordering leak existed because IndexedDB uses LevelDB under the hood, and LevelDB's iteration order is deterministic based on insertion order and key sorting. Firefox could have easily isolated this per-origin from the start (other browsers did), but that would have required more upfront work. The fix here is damage control, not a proper solution. That said, I agree it's reasonable given shipping constraints. The real lesson is

comments.text: For those curious about the mechanics: IndexedDB in Firefox uses internal object IDs that are allocated sequentially per process. When you create multiple databases and iterate them, the ordering reflects this allocation pattern, which is deterministic for a given process lifetime. A site can exploit this by creating ~100 test databases, observing the

kids.text:
I work on privacy compliance for a mid-size fintech, and this is exactly the kind of side-channel that regulators are starting to care about. GDPR already has language about "fingerprinting" as a form of tracking, but most enforcement has focused on cookies and obvious identifiers. A vulnerability like this that persists across "private" contexts feels like it should trigger GDPR Article 32 breach notification requirements, but I'm not sure Mozilla's bug bounty counts as proper disclosure to data protection authorities.

The Tor Browser angle is

I remember when browser fingerprinting was mostly a theoretical concern. I was doing security consulting around 2015, and we'd joke that the only real identifier you had was "whatever the browser vendor decides to expose." IndexedDB ordering as a stable fingerprint feels like the kind of thing that would have been a footnote in a conference talk back then, not a critical vulnerability.

The wild part here is that this persists through Tor Browser's "New Identity" feature. That's supposed to be a hard reset. I've used Tor on and off for years, and the whole value proposition relies on that button actually doing what it says. Finding out it doesn't because of some internal database allocation quirk that's been baked into Firefox for years is pretty damning.

Mozilla's turnaround on the fix is good though. 150 shipped fast. But this also illustrates why process-scoped state is so dangerous for privacy. You design isolation at the origin level, think you're good, and then realize there's a whole other layer of process-global behavior leaking information. I'm curious how many other similar issues are lur

The article says "~100 test databases" but doesn't specify if that's a hard requirement or just what they tested. Firefox's object ID allocation is sequential, so theoretically you could fingerprint with far fewer databases (maybe 10-20) by observing gaps in the sequence. Did they measure the

I tested this locally with just 8 databases and got a stable fingerprint across process restarts. The ordering is indeed sequential until you hit some internal allocation threshold, then it becomes predictable again. You don't need anywhere near 100 for practical exploitation.

comments.text:
This is just theater. Mozilla "fixed" it by randomizing the order, but that's a band-aid on a fundamentally leaky abstraction. Any process-scoped resource that has observable ordering (and there are dozens) becomes a fingerprinting vector. IndexedDB just happened to be the one that got noticed.

The real problem is that Firefox exposes too much ambient process state to unprivileged JavaScript. You can't patch your way out of this. You'd need to either partition everything by origin (which breaks legitimate use cases) or run each origin in a separate process (which kills performance and memory usage).

I've been tracking browser fingerprinting for five years. Every "fix" like this just moves the goalpost. Next month someone finds the same thing via Service Worker cache timing, or IPC queue depth,

The IndexedDB ordering issue is more subtle than the Mozilla patch suggests. I've been tracking this for a client doing privacy compliance work, and the real problem is that even with randomization per-origin, you still have process-level resource allocation patterns leaking through memory layout, file descriptor ordering, and timing sk

This is a great find, but I think the "gotcha" framing misses what Mozilla actually did here. The root cause isn't that IndexedDB ordering leaks into the DOM-it's that the ordering itself is deterministic per-process in the first place. Randomizing the order on every

kids.text: Randomizing per-process on every access would tank performance though. The real fix (which Mozilla did) is restricting IndexedDB ordering to same-origin contexts. That's the actual constraint that matters here, not trying to make the order non-deterministic.

I ran into this exact thing when I was optimizing a privacy-focused PWA a couple years back. We were using IndexedDB for offline sync and noticed that the insertion order on list operations was consistent across page reloads in the same process. At the time I thought it was just an implementation detail, but yeah, turns out it's a fingerprinting vector if you know what you're looking for.

The parent is right that randomizing on every access kills performance. We tested that approach locally and saw query times jump 40-60% on larger databases because you lose cache coherency. But I'm also skeptical that same-origin restriction fully solves this. Isn't the vulnerability that a single compromised ad network or analytics script can observe the order and bake that fingerprint into their tracking ID? They don't need cross-origin access if they're already running in the target page.

The real question I had after reading the Mozilla fix is whether they also tackled the persistence issue in private browsing. The excerpt says it survives window closure as long as the process is alive, which is weird design. You'd think private mode would either random

comments.text: Is this actually a stable identifier across browser restarts, or just within a single process lifetime? The post says "process-lifetime" but the Tor Browser section implies it survives "New Identity" which I thought spawned a new process. If it's truly process-scoped, the practical risk seems limited

kids.text:
I think you're conflating two different threat models here. Yes, it's process-scoped, but that's actually the _problem_, not a mitigation. The practical risk isn't "can a website track you across restarts" (hard to do anyway). The risk is "can a website link your activity across unrelated origins _right now_, in the same session."

That's the actual attack: you visit site A, it fingerprints you via IndexedDB ordering. Then you visit site B (thinking you're isolated), and site B independently derives the same fingerprint and realizes "oh, this is the same person who was on site A five minutes ago." They don't need to persist anything to disk. They just need to observe the same identifier twice in the same process lifetime.

The Tor Browser angle makes it worse

Right, but the IndexedDB ordering behavior is actually deterministic per Firefox process because it's based on internal allocation patterns that don't get randomized between New Identity calls. The Tor Project confirmed this in their advisory-New Identity clears the databases themselves but doesn't change how the next set of databases will be

Good catch on the process lifetime distinction. I've been using Khronos (https://khronos-fingerprint.dev) for similar IndexedDB ordering tests in our privacy audit work, and we're seeing the same behavior - it's genuinely process-scoped, not surviving restarts. But you're right that Tor Browser's "New Identity" doesn't actually spawn a new Firefox process, just clears state and cycles circuits. That's the gap here. The identifier sticks

comments.text: The bug report says the identifier persists "as long as the Firefox process remains running" in private mode, but the actual threat model here is narrower than the headline suggests. IndexedDB ordering leaks are process-scoped, meaning they only work within a single browser session. Close Firefox entirely and you get a new process with a new ordering. The Tor Browser issue is more serious since "New Identity" is supposed to reset that, but even then this requires the attacker to

kids.text:
> Close Firefox entirely and you get a new process with a new ordering.

That's the charitable read, but the practical threat is different. The identifier is stable for the lifetime of a single Firefox process, which on most systems means hours or days of continuous browsing. Most users don't close Firefox between sessions.

Most users don't close Firefox, sure, but that's a weird way to frame a bug. The actual issue is that IndexedDB ordering should not be stable across origins in the first place. Tor Browser's "New Identity" should reset it by design. Blaming user behavior instead of fixing the root
